title: NOC 94203 – Assemblers, fabricators and inspectors, industrial electrical motors and transformers | GTR Immigration
description: In 2026, as Canada accelerates its "Green Grid" initiatives and the electrification of heavy industry, Industrial Electrical Motor and Transformer Assemblers (NOC 94203) are among the most strategically important manufacturing professionals. These technicians build, wind, and test the massive power transformers and high-efficiency motors that drive Canada’s mines, hydroelectric plants, and new EV battery gigafactories. As the industry shifts toward "Smart Transformers" equipped with real-time diagnostic sensors, international workers with experience in precision coil winding and high-voltage testing are seeing a significant surge in demand.

classification.teer: FeatureDetailsBroad Occupational Category9 – Occupations in manufacturing and utilitiesTEER Category4 (Occupations usually requiring secondary school and on-the-job training)Major Group94 – Machine operators, assemblers and inspectorsSub-Major Group942 – Assemblers and inspectors in manufacturing

duties: Coil Winding: Operating specialized machines to wind copper wire or foil onto cores to create high-precision transformer and motor windings.
Core Assembly: Stacking and insulating laminations of silicon steel to form the electromagnetic cores of heavy electrical apparatus.
Component Installation: Installing and connecting complex internal wiring, bushings, tap changers, and cooling systems according to electrical schematics.
High-Voltage Testing: Conducting dielectric, resistance, and load tests using advanced oscilloscopes and power analyzers to ensure equipment meets international standards.
2026 Update: Calibrating IoT sensors within "Smart Transformers" that monitor internal heat and dissolved gases for predictive maintenance.
employment_requirements: Education: Completion of secondary school is the standard requirement.
Training: On-the-job training is provided; however, a College Certificate in Electrical Techniques or Power Engineering Technology is a major asset.
Experience: Previous experience as a labourer in an electrical or heavy manufacturing plant is often required for advancement.
Safety: Mandatory 2026 WHMIS and specialized "High-Voltage Safety for Manufacturers" certification.
job_outlook: The Canadian Occupational Projection System (COPS) expects a "Balanced" market with localized shortages in manufacturing hubs. The complexity of 2026-grade renewable energy transformers requires a new generation of Inspectors and Testers with digital troubleshooting skills. Over 3,800 openings are projected due to the modernization of North America’s power grid.
labour_market_demand: The 2026 outlook is characterized by "Critical Growth" as Canada secures its domestic supply chain for electrical infrastructure:
Ontario (Hamilton & London): Very High demand. This region is a historic hub for heavy electrical manufacturing, currently expanding to support the North American EV supply chain.
Quebec (Varennes & Saint-Bruno): Strong demand. Home to global leaders in transformer technology, Quebec is actively recruiting for major provincial energy projects.
Alberta (Edmonton/Calgary): Good prospects. Driven by the modernization of the electrical grid and the integration of renewable energy storage systems.
National Trend: Approximately 12,300 people work in this occupation. The market favors those who can handle the physical requirements of heavy assembly alongside digital testing protocols.
Why Work in Canada as a Transformer/Motor Assembler?
Infrastructure Priority: You are building the literal "backbone" of the Canadian energy transition, ensuring high job security through 2033.
High-Tech Heavy Industry: Work with 2026-grade technologies, including automated vertical winding machines, laser-guided core stacking, and computerized oil-purification systems.
Path to PR: Frequently prioritized in Provincial Nominee Program (PNP) "In-Demand" lists and is a core qualifier for the 2026 TR to PR transition programs.
Specialized Skill Set: Unlike general assembly, the technical nature of high-voltage coil winding often leads to higher wages and faster internal promotion.
pathways: TR to PR Pathway (April 2026): Motor and transformer assemblers are classified as "Essential Manufacturing Workers," making them high-priority candidates for this 33,000-person transition quota.
Ontario Employer Job Offer (In-Demand Skills): This NOC is frequently eligible for provincial nomination for those with a valid job offer in Ontario’s industrial sectors.
OINP Express Entry Human Capital Priorities: Periodically targets candidates with experience in specialized electrical and power manufacturing sectors.
Saskatchewan Hard-to-Fill Skills Pilot: A streamlined pathway for technical manufacturing workers to obtain Permanent Residency with a provincial job offer.
